title: The shape of data: the histogram
intro: Points on a line work while the numbers are few. But the "Mama John's" chain has grown to fifty employees — individual points no longer tell you anything. You need to see the whole shape: where salaries are dense, where sparse, whether a tail stretches out anywhere. So the data is dropped into "bins" — ranges — counting how many people fall into each. That is a histogram.

beats.text: The height of a bar is how many people earn a salary from that range. The main thing is visible at once: there is a "hump" where most people are (around 33–37k) and thin edges. That is the shape of the data — in a single glance.
Bin width is a choice, and it changes the picture.
beats.predict: Play with the "number of bins". What happens to the shape when there are too few bins, or too many?
beats.reveal: Too few bins — everything merges into a couple of bars and the shape is lost. Too many — nearly every value gets its own bar, and you see noise instead of shape. The truth is in the middle: enough bars to make out the hump and the tails.

beats.text: The shape shows not only the "hump" but the tails too.
beats.predict: Press "add the director" with their big salary. Where will their bar land — next to the hump or far off to the side?
beats.reveal: Far to the right, a separate bar torn away from the bulk. That is what a long right tail looks like on a histogram: the data is skewed, rare large values stretch to the right. A "lonely bar in the distance" is the first visual sign of an outlier; what to do with it is covered in a separate lesson on outliers.

beats.text: The shape immediately reveals the character of the data: a symmetric "bell" (values hug the center, the tails on both sides are equal), a skewed distribution with a long tail to one side (salaries, prices, response times), or several "humps" — a sign that different groups are mixed in the data. One picture answers: where it concentrates, whether the spread is wide, whether there is skew and lone values.

That is why the histogram is the first thing an analyst looks at upon opening a new column of numbers: it shows the whole shape, before any means or models. And to describe the edges of the distribution precisely — what share of the data lies below a given threshold — percentiles and quartiles come in handy. That is what we'll take up next.

definitions.text: A bar chart of the shape of data: values are split into ranges (bins) and it shows how many observations fall into each. It reveals where the data concentrates and where it is sparse.
definitions.simple: data sorted into buckets: where it is dense and where it is empty.

definitions.text: A single range of values on a histogram. Bin width is a choice: too wide smooths the shape away, too narrow shows random noise instead of shape.
definitions.simple: a range values fall into; the shape is built from the bins.

definitions.text: A region of rare but far-lying values on one side of a distribution. On a histogram — low bars stretching far from the main hump.
definitions.simple: rare large values that pull the edge of the distribution sideways.

practice: Shape is the first diagnosis of data. The salary histogram of "Mama John's" shows at once both the typical level (a hump around 33–37k) and the skew, when a lonely bar for the director appears on the right.

So before computing means and building models, an analyst always looks at the shape: it hints whether the data is symmetric, whether there is a long tail, and whether lone values might distort the conclusions.
realLife: Histograms are everywhere: the distribution of grades in a class, app load times, audience ages, store receipts. The shape instantly tells you whether the data is "even" or long-tailed.
The shape also suggests the method: for a symmetric "bell" the mean and σ work well, while for skewed, tailed data the median and percentiles are more honest.

We keep mentioning the "hump" and the "long tail". But data is convenient to describe not only by shape but also by its edges — what share of values lies below a given threshold. That is what we'll take up in the next lesson.
nextLabel: How to describe data by its edges rather than its center? → Percentiles and quartiles
